serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of fried macaroni with eggs is approximately 300 g, typically presented as a single plate at a hawker stall or casual dining restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Macaroni with Egg — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods: Macaroni cooked enriched (FDC ID 20121) and Scrambled egg (FDC ID 01132), combined in representative proportions (70% cooked macaroni, 20% scrambled egg, 10% oil and seasonings), with sodium adjusted for soy sauce addition.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for fried macaroni with eggs found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values estimated from USDA SR Legacy component foods: cooked enriched macaroni (FDC 20121) and scrambled egg (FDC 01132), blended in proportions representative of a typical Vietnamese-style fried macaroni dish (approximately 70 g cooked macaroni, 20 g egg, 10 g oil and seasonings per 100 g final dish). Sodium elevated to reflect soy sauce and seasoning additions typical of the dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — pasta (macaroni, white, boiled) GI = 47 (glucose reference); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care — American Diabetes Association peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for fried macaroni with eggs. The GI of 47 is taken from the peer-reviewed international GI tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) for white macaroni boiled, which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Stir-frying with egg and oil does not substantially raise the GI of the pasta component; the egg and oil contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 70.5 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 ≈ 33.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 300 g serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, which represents over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced soy sauce are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined macaroni carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of 47 (low) but a glycemic load of approximately 33 per standard serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a plate and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat and sodium content with cholesterol from eggs
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains approximately 21.6 g total fat and 285 mg cholesterol per 300 g serving, along with elevated sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and request preparation with minimal oil and reduced soy sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried macaroni with eggs (Vietnamese: Nui chiên trứng) is a stir-fried pasta dish popular in Singapore's Vietnamese restaurant scene, particularly at establishments such as Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant. The dish consists of cooked elbow macaroni that is wok-tossed or pan-fried with beaten eggs, garlic, spring onions, soy sauce, and cooking oil until the egg is set and lightly golden, coating the pasta in a savoury, umami-rich glaze. The texture is soft with slightly crispy edges where the egg has caramelised against the hot pan. The flavour profile is savoury and mildly salty, with the egg providing richness and the soy sauce contributing depth. Nutritionally, a standard 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 555 kcal, 20 g protein, 21.6 g fat, and 70.5 g carbohydrate. The dish has a low glycemic index of 47 due to the pasta's dense starch structure, but the glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 33, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce, making this a moderate concern for hypertensive individuals. The dish is vegetarian and halal-friendly when prepared without meat additions. It is commonly served as a main course or a light meal and is a comfort food staple in Vietnamese-influenced Singaporean casual dining.

How many calories in fried macaroni with eggs?

confidence_reason: No exact match for fried macaroni with eggs was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional database. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked enriched macaroni and scrambled egg in representative proportions. GI is taken from the closest analogous food (boiled white macaroni) in the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-based estimation methodology is transparent and defensible, and the GI source is peer-reviewed.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked enriched macaroni (FDC 20121) and scrambled egg (FDC 01132), blended at approximately 70:20:10 (macaroni:egg:oil+seasoning) by weight. Sodium is elevated to 420 mg/100 g to reflect soy sauce addition typical of this dish style. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as the dish uses standard vegetable cooking oil with negligible industrial trans fat; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for the component oils. 3. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for boiled white macaroni; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated from component oil profile), polyunsaturated_fat_g estimated similarly. 5. The dish is served at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant on Circular Road, Singapore, at SGD 15.00 per plate. 6. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for fried macaroni with eggs as a prepared dish. 7. Halal status is assumed based on typical preparation without pork; individual restaurant practices should be verified. 8. Vietnamese local name 'Nui chiên trứng' is the canonical Vietnamese-language name for this dish and is preserved from the input record.
